question: A ceiling fan in a classroom is initially at rest with one blade pointing directly towards the door. After the fan rotates through a certain angle, the blade now points towards the window. Which physical quantity best describes the angle through which the blade has rotated from its initial position to its final position?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify the physical quantity that measures the angle through which a rotating object changes its position about a fixed axis.
explanation.principle:
Circular motion involves the movement of an object about a fixed axis or centre.

Before studying how fast or how quickly an object rotates, we must first know how far it has rotated.

The quantity used to describe this rotational change in position is called angular displacement.

Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates from its initial position to its final position.

It is measured in degrees (°) or, in the SI system, radians (rad).

Do not confuse angular displacement with other rotational quantities:

• Angular velocity describes how fast the angle changes with time.
• Angular acceleration describes how angular velocity changes with time.
• Centripetal force is the inward force that keeps an object moving in a circular path.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking.

The question is concerned with the angle through which the fan blade has moved from its starting position to its final position.

Step 2: Recall the definition.

The angle through which a rotating body changes its position is called angular displacement.

Step 3: Match the definition with the options.

Option A correctly names this quantity as angular displacement.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because angular velocity measures the rate of change of angular displacement with time, not the angular change itself.
Option C is incorrect because angular acceleration measures how angular velocity changes with time.
Option D is incorrect because centripetal force is the inward force responsible for maintaining circular motion and does not describe rotational position.
explanation.exam_tip:
When beginning Circular Motion, first distinguish between the four basic rotational quantities:

• Angular displacement → how far an object rotates.
• Angular velocity → how fast it rotates.
• Angular acceleration → how its rotational speed changes.
• Centripetal force → the inward force that keeps it moving in a circular path.

Many JAMB questions test whether candidates can correctly identify these quantities before attempting calculations.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine turning the steering wheel of a car. The angle through which you turn the wheel from its starting position is called the angular displacement. It simply tells you how far the wheel has rotated.

question: A standing electric fan completes one full revolution of its blades while operating in a living room. What is the angular displacement of the blades during this revolution?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the angular displacement corresponding to one complete revolution of a rotating object such as the blades of a standing electric fan.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

One complete revolution means the object has rotated once and returned to its starting position after covering a full circular path.

The standard relationships are:

• 1 complete revolution = 360°
• 1 complete revolution = 2π radians

Whenever the question asks for the angular displacement after one full revolution in degrees, the answer is always 360°.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the number of revolutions.

The fan blades complete one full revolution.

Step 2: Recall the relationship.

1 complete revolution = 360°.

Step 3: Calculate the angular displacement.

Angular displacement = 1 × 360° = 360°.

Step 4: Compare with the options.

360° corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (90°) represents one-quarter of a complete revolution, not a full revolution.
Option B (180°) represents half a revolution, not a complete revolution.
Option D (720°) represents two complete revolutions rather than one.
explanation.exam_tip:
A common JAMB question is to relate the number of revolutions to angular displacement.

Memorise these important facts:

• 1 revolution = 360° = 2π radians.
• Half a revolution = 180° = π radians.
• Quarter of a revolution = 90° = π/2 radians.

Knowing these basic conversions makes many Circular Motion questions straightforward.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine placing a sticker on one fan blade. As the blade rotates once and returns to the exact position where it started, it has completed one full turn. One full turn is an angular displacement of 360°.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Angular Displacement to learn how angular displacement is measured in degrees, radians and revolutions, with worked JAMB-style examples and practical applications involving fans, wheels, turbines and rotating machines.

question: A grinding machine in a neighbourhood workshop completes 7 full revolutions while sharpening a metal rod. What is the angular displacement of the grinding wheel in degrees?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the angular displacement of a rotating grinding wheel after it completes a given number of full revolutions.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

For complete revolutions, each revolution corresponds to a full turn of 360°.

Therefore:

Angular displacement = Number of revolutions × 360°

Before using the formula, identify:

• the number of complete revolutions;
• the required unit (degrees in this case).

Since every complete revolution covers 360°, multiplying the number of revolutions by 360° gives the total angular displacement.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known quantity.

Number of revolutions = 7.

Step 2: Recall the relationship.

1 revolution = 360°.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

Angular displacement = 7 × 360°.

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

Angular displacement = 2,520°.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

2,520° corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (1,260°) is incorrect because it corresponds to only 3.5 revolutions, not 7 revolutions.
Option B (2,160°) is incorrect because it represents 6 complete revolutions.
Option D (2,880°) is incorrect because it represents 8 complete revolutions.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question gives the number of complete revolutions and asks for angular displacement in degrees, simply multiply the number of revolutions by 360°. Always check the unit requested before choosing your answer.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine drawing a full circle every time the grinding wheel turns once. Each full circle measures 360°. After 7 complete turns, the wheel has rotated through 7 × 360° = 2,520°.

question: A Ferris wheel at a recreational park rotates through three-quarters of a complete revolution as it carries passengers from the boarding point to another position. What is the angular displacement of the cabin in degrees?

explanation.question_restate: The question requires you to determine the angular displacement corresponding to three-quarters of one complete revolution of a Ferris wheel.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

One complete revolution corresponds to 360°.

Therefore, the angular displacement for any fraction of a revolution is obtained by multiplying that fraction by 360°.

Formula:

Angular displacement = Fraction of a revolution × 360°
steps:
Step 1: Identify the given information.

Fraction of revolution = 3/4.
Step 2: Recall that one complete revolution equals 360°.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

Angular displacement = (3/4) × 360°

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

Angular displacement = 270°.

Step 5: Compare your result with the options.

270° corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (180°) represents half of a complete revolution, not three-quarters.
Option B (240°) is obtained from an incorrect multiplication and does not correspond to three-quarters of 360°.
Option D (300°) exceeds three-quarters of a revolution and is not the correct angular displacement.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always remember the standard rotational equivalents:

• 1 revolution = 360°
• 1/2 revolution = 180°
• 1/4 revolution = 90°
• 3/4 revolution = 270°

JAMB frequently tests these simple rotational relationships before introducing radian conversions.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine dividing a complete circle into four equal parts. Each part is 90°.

Three-quarters of the circle means moving through three of those parts:

3 × 90° = 270°.

Therefore, the cabin has an angular displacement of 270°.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Angular Displacement to learn how to calculate angular displacement from complete and fractional revolutions, convert between degrees and radians, and solve additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question:
A bicycle mechanic observes that a wheel has rotated through an angle of 150° while testing the brake system. What is the angular displacement of the wheel in radians?

(Take π ≈ 3.142.)

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to convert an angular displacement measured in degrees into its equivalent value in radians.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

Angles may be expressed in degrees or radians.

The radian is the SI unit of angular measurement and is widely used in physics because many rotational equations are expressed in radians.

The relationship between degrees and radians is:

180° = π radians

Therefore,

Angle in radians = Angle in degrees × (π / 180).

Whenever a question asks you to convert degrees to radians, simply multiply the given angle by π/180 and simplify the fraction if possible.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the given angle.

Angular displacement = 150°.

Step 2: Use the conversion formula.

Angle in radians = 150 × (π / 180).

Step 3: Simplify the fraction.

150/180 = 5/6.

Therefore,

Angular displacement = (5π/6) rad.

Step 4: Express the answer in decimal form.

5/6 = 0.833 (to three decimal places).

Hence,

Angular displacement ≈ 0.833π rad.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option B matches the calculated value.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because 0.417π rad corresponds approximately to 75°, not 150°.
Option C is incorrect because 1.200π rad corresponds to about 216°, which is greater than the given angle.
Option D is incorrect because 1.500π rad is equivalent to 270°, not 150°.
explanation.exam_tip:
Memorise these essential conversions:

• 180° = π rad
• 360° = 2π rad

For degree-to-radian conversion, always multiply by π/180. Simplify the fraction before converting to decimal whenever possible.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of degrees and radians as two different ways of measuring the same rotation, just like metres and kilometres measure the same distance.

Since 180° equals π radians, 150° is:

150 × (π/180) = 5π/6 ≈ 0.833π rad.

Therefore, Option B is correct.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly explanations and additional practice questions.

question: The wheel of a motorcycle rotates through an angular displacement of \(\frac{5\pi}{6}\) radians while the motorcycle is being pushed into a repair garage. What is this angular displacement in degrees?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to convert an angular displacement expressed in radians into its equivalent value in degrees.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

Angles can be measured in either degrees or radians.

A complete revolution is equal to:

• 360°
• 2π radians

From this relationship,

π radians = 180°.

Therefore, to convert radians to degrees, multiply the angle in radians by 180/π.

The conversion formula is:

Angle in degrees = Angle in radians × (180/π)

where:

• π is approximately 3.142.
• The angle in radians is the given angular displacement.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the given angular displacement.

Angular displacement = 5π/6 radians.

Step 2: Recall the conversion formula.

Angle in degrees = Angle in radians × (180/π).

Step 3: Substitute the given value.

Angle = (5π/6) × (180/π).

Step 4: Cancel π from the numerator and denominator.

Angle = (5 × 180)/6.

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

Angle = 900/6 = 150°.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (120°) results from an incorrect conversion or arithmetic error.
Option C (210°) is greater than the correct converted value and may arise from using an incorrect relationship between radians and degrees.
Option D (300°) is obtained by an incorrect multiplication and does not correspond to 5π/6 radians.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always remember these important relationships:

• 180° = π radians
• 360° = 2π radians

Whenever converting radians to degrees, multiply by 180/π. When converting degrees to radians, multiply by π/180.

JAMB frequently tests these conversions directly before introducing more advanced circular motion calculations.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of radians and degrees as two different units for measuring the same angle, just as metres and kilometres measure length.

Since π radians represent 180°, multiplying by 180/π converts any angle from radians into degrees.

Applying this to 5π/6 radians gives 150°.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and revision.

question: A BRT bus travelling along a Lagos route has a wheel that makes 9 complete revolutions before the bus reaches the next bus stop. What is the angular displacement of the wheel during this interval in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the angular displacement of a rotating wheel from the number of complete revolutions it makes.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates about a fixed axis.

For rotational motion:

• One complete revolution = 360°
• One complete revolution = 2π radians.

Therefore,

Angular displacement = Number of revolutions × 2π radians.

Radians are the SI unit for measuring angular displacement and are commonly used in calculations involving circular motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Number of revolutions = 9.

Step 2: Recall the relationship.

Angular displacement = Number of revolutions × 2π.

Step 3: Substitute the given value.

Angular displacement = 9 × 2π.

Step 4: Simplify the expression.

Angular displacement = 18π rad.

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

18π rad corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it represents only half of the required angular displacement.
Option B is incorrect because it corresponds to 6 complete revolutions, not 9.
Option D is incorrect because it is twice the correct angular displacement and would correspond to 18 complete revolutions.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question gives the number of complete revolutions and asks for angular displacement in radians, immediately multiply the number of revolutions by 2π. Do not convert through degrees unless specifically required.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of one full turn of a wheel as an angle of 2π radians. If the wheel turns 9 times, the total angle turned is simply 9 × 2π = 18π radians.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Angular Displacement to learn how to convert between revolutions, degrees and radians, with worked JAMB-style examples and additional practice in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
A water pumping machine is undergoing maintenance. During a test run, the drive shaft is observed to rotate through an angular displacement of 30π radians.

How many complete revolutions does the shaft make during the test?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the number of complete revolutions made by a rotating shaft when its angular displacement is given in radians.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement describes the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis.

A complete revolution corresponds to one full turn.

The standard relationship is:

1 complete revolution = 2π radians.

Whenever the angular displacement is given in radians and the number of revolutions is required, divide the angular displacement by 2π.

Formula:

Number of revolutions = Angular displacement ÷ 2π
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Angular displacement, θ = 30π radians.

Step 2: State the required quantity.

Number of revolutions, n.

Step 3: Apply the relationship.

n = θ ÷ 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given value.

n = 30π ÷ 2π

Step 5: Simplify.

n = 15.

Therefore, the shaft completes 15 revolutions.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it underestimates the number of revolutions obtained from the given angular displacement.
Option C is incorrect because it results from an incorrect conversion between radians and revolutions.
Option D is incorrect because it wrongly assumes the numerical coefficient of π is the number of revolutions without dividing by 2π.
explanation.exam_tip:
For questions involving revolutions and radians, remember the fundamental conversion:

1 revolution = 2π radians.

If the angular displacement is in radians and the question asks for the number of revolutions, always divide by 2π.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of one complete turn of the shaft as one revolution. Since one revolution is equal to 2π radians, 30π radians contain:

30π ÷ 2π = 15

So the shaft makes 15 complete turns.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Angular Displacement to learn how to convert between revolutions, degrees and radians, solve reverse-conversion problems, and practise additional JAMB-style questions on rotational motion.

question:
A coach instructs athletes to complete one full lap of a standard circular running track and return to the exact point where they started.

Which statement correctly compares the athlete's linear distance travelled with the angular displacement about the centre of the track after the lap?

options.A: The distance travelled is zero, while the angular displacement is one complete revolution.
options.B: The distance travelled equals the radius of the track, while the angular displacement is zero.
options.C: The distance travelled equals the circumference of the track, while the angular displacement is one complete revolution (360° or 2π radians).
options.D: Both the distance travelled and the angular displacement are zero because the athlete returns to the starting point.

answer_text: The distance travelled equals the circumference of the track, while the angular displacement is one complete revolution (360° or 2π radians).

explanation.question_restate: This question compares two different ways of describing motion around a circular path. It tests whether you can distinguish linear distance travelled from angular displacement after one complete revolution.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement describes the angle through which an object rotates about a fixed centre. It does not measure the length of the path.

Linear distance, on the other hand, measures the actual length of the path covered.

For one complete revolution:

• Linear distance travelled = circumference of the circle.
• Angular displacement = 360° = 2π radians = one complete revolution.

Although the athlete returns to the starting point, the body has completed an entire rotation about the centre of the track. Therefore, the angular displacement is one full revolution, while the distance travelled is the circumference.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what is meant by linear distance.

The athlete runs along the entire circular track.

Therefore, the linear distance travelled equals the circumference of the track.

Step 2: Determine the angular displacement.

One complete lap corresponds to one complete revolution about the centre.

Hence, the angular displacement is 360° or 2π radians.

Step 3: Compare both quantities.

Linear distance and angular displacement describe different aspects of the same motion.

The correct comparison is that the athlete travels one circumference while rotating through one complete revolution.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Therefore, Option C is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the athlete covers the entire circumference of the track. The distance travelled cannot be zero.
Option B is incorrect because the distance travelled is not equal to the radius, and the angular displacement after one full lap is not zero.
Option D is incorrect because returning to the starting point does not mean no motion occurred. The athlete travelled one circumference and completed one full revolution.
explanation.exam_tip:
Do not confuse linear quantities with angular quantities.

Distance measures the length of the path travelled, whereas angular displacement measures the angle through which an object rotates.

In circular motion, one complete revolution always corresponds to an angular displacement of 360° (2π radians), regardless of the size of the circle.
explanation.final_answer: C. The distance travelled equals the circumference of the track, while the angular displacement is one complete revolution (360° or 2π radians).
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine walking once around a circular field.

You have walked all the way around the edge, so the distance you travelled is the circumference.

At the same time, you have turned completely around the centre once, giving an angular displacement of one complete revolution (360° or 2π radians).

question:
The minute hand of a wall clock is initially pointing at 12. It rotates clockwise until it points at 4.

Taking clockwise rotation as negative, what is the angular displacement of the minute hand?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to determine the angular displacement of a rotating object while correctly interpreting the direction of rotation. The minute hand moves clockwise from 12 to 4, and the sign convention for clockwise rotation is applied.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates from its initial position to its final position about a fixed axis.

A complete revolution is 360° or 2π radians.

A wall clock is divided into 12 equal sectors. Therefore, the angle between two consecutive numbers is:

360° ÷ 12 = 30°.

When a sign convention is specified:

• Anticlockwise rotation is taken as positive.
• Clockwise rotation is taken as negative.

Always identify both the magnitude of rotation and its direction before assigning the sign.
steps:
Step 1: Determine the angle between adjacent numbers on the clock.

Each interval represents 30°.

Step 2: Count the clockwise movement.

From 12 to 4 is four equal intervals:

12 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4

Number of intervals = 4.

Step 3: Calculate the angular displacement.

Angular displacement = 4 × 30° = 120°.

Step 4: Apply the sign convention.

Since the rotation is clockwise and clockwise is defined as negative, the angular displacement is -120°.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option A is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it gives the correct magnitude but assigns the wrong sign. The question states that clockwise rotation is negative.
Option C is incorrect because 240° corresponds to eight clock intervals, not four.
Option D is incorrect because it has both an incorrect magnitude and the wrong sign.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a clock-based angular displacement question appears, first determine the number of intervals moved, multiply by 30°, and then apply any stated sign convention. Read the question carefully because JAMB may specify whether clockwise or anticlockwise rotation is taken as positive.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of the clock as a circle divided into 12 equal parts. Moving from one number to the next is 30°. From 12 to 4 is four intervals, so the angle is 120°. Since the movement is clockwise and the question defines clockwise as negative, the answer is -120°.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice.

question: A turbine at a hydroelectric power station starts from a marked reference position and rotates through 7 complete revolutions before maintenance engineers stop it for inspection. What is the angular displacement of the turbine during this rotation in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the angular displacement of a rotating turbine after it completes a given number of full revolutions and express the answer in radians.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

For rotational motion, one complete revolution corresponds to:

• 360°
• 2π radians

Whenever the number of complete revolutions is known, the angular displacement can be obtained by multiplying the number of revolutions by 2π radians.

Thus,

Angular displacement = Number of revolutions × 2π radians.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Number of revolutions = 7.

Step 2: Recall the relationship between revolutions and radians.

1 revolution = 2π radians.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

Angular displacement = 7 × 2π
= 14π radians.

Step 4: Compare the calculated value with the available options.

14π rad corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it assumes only π rad per revolution instead of 2π rad.
Option B is incorrect because 12π rad corresponds to six complete revolutions, not seven.
Option D is incorrect because 21π rad results from an incorrect multiplication and does not represent seven complete revolutions.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always remember the fundamental conversion:

• 1 revolution = 360° = 2π radians.

For questions involving complete revolutions, simply multiply the number of revolutions by 2π. Avoid converting through degrees unless specifically required.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine placing a coloured sticker on one blade of the turbine. Each time the sticker returns to its starting point, the turbine has completed one full revolution, which is equal to 2π radians.

After 7 complete revolutions, the total angular displacement is:

7 × 2π = 14π radians.

question: In a food processing factory, an industrial mixer starts from its reference position and completes 7 full revolutions before stopping for inspection. What is the total angular displacement of the mixer drum in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the angular displacement of an industrial mixer after completing several full revolutions and express the result in radians.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

A complete revolution corresponds to one full turn.

The standard relationships are:

• 1 revolution = 360°
• 1 revolution = 2π radians

Whenever the number of complete revolutions is given, the angular displacement in radians is obtained by multiplying the number of revolutions by 2π.

Thus,

Angular displacement = Number of revolutions × 2π radians.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Number of revolutions = 7.

Step 2: Recall the conversion.

1 revolution = 2π radians.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

Angular displacement = 7 × 2π
= 14π radians.

Step 4: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The correct option is 14π rad.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (7π rad) is incorrect because it represents only half of the required angular displacement.
Option B (12π rad) is incorrect because it corresponds to 6 complete revolutions, not 7.
Option D (21π rad) is incorrect because it overestimates the angular displacement and does not correspond to 7 complete revolutions.
explanation.exam_tip: For questions involving complete revolutions, first remember that one revolution is equal to 2π radians. Multiply the number of revolutions directly by 2π unless the question involves only a fraction of a revolution.
explanation.simple_explanation: Each complete turn contributes 2π radians. Since the mixer makes 7 complete turns, its total angular displacement is 7 × 2π = 14π radians.

question: A conveyor roller in a bottling company rotates 9/4 complete revolutions while transporting bottles along the production line. What is the angular displacement of the roller in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question asks you to determine the angular displacement, expressed in radians, of a conveyor roller that makes 9/4 complete revolutions.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what angular displacement means.

Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates from its starting position to its final position about a fixed axis. It tells us how much the object has turned.

Unlike linear displacement, which measures movement along a straight or curved path, angular displacement measures rotational movement.

Angular displacement is important because it forms the foundation for studying angular velocity and angular acceleration.

One complete revolution corresponds to a full circle.

A full circle measures:
• 360°
• 2π radians

Since radians are the SI unit of angular measurement, they are commonly used in Physics calculations.

The fundamental relationship is:

Angular displacement, θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

where:
• θ = angular displacement (radians)
• Number of revolutions = total complete and fractional turns made
• π ≈ 3.142

This is a fundamental conversion formula. Later in Circular Motion, this angular displacement will be used to calculate angular velocity using:

ω = θ/t

where ω is angular velocity and t is the time taken.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known value.

Number of revolutions = 9/4.

Step 2: Identify the quantity required.

Required: Angular displacement in radians.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given value.

θ = (9/4) × 2π

Step 5: Simplify the expression.

θ = (18π)/4

θ = 9π/2 rad

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

Option B gives 9π/2 rad.

Therefore, Option B is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because 3π/2 rad corresponds to only 3/4 of a revolution, not 9/4 revolutions.
Option C is incorrect because it results from multiplying by π instead of 2π, a common beginner's mistake.
Option D is incorrect because 5π rad represents 2.5 revolutions, which is greater than the given 9/4 revolutions.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question gives the number of revolutions and asks for angular displacement in radians, immediately remember that one complete revolution equals 2π radians. Multiply the number of revolutions by 2π before simplifying your answer.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to multiply the number of revolutions by π instead of 2π. Always remember that a complete revolution is a full circle, which is equal to 2π radians, not π radians.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine placing a sticker on a conveyor roller. Each time the sticker returns to its starting position, the roller has completed one full revolution, which is equal to 2π radians. If the roller turns 9/4 revolutions, you simply multiply 9/4 by 2π to obtain 9π/2 radians.

question: A wind energy technician observes that the rotor of a wind turbine completes 15 full revolutions while the control system is being calibrated. What is the angular displacement of the rotor during this interval in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the angular displacement of a rotating object from the number of complete revolutions it makes. Here, the rotating object is the rotor of a wind turbine.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

Unlike linear distance, angular displacement describes rotational motion.

Before solving questions on angular displacement, remember these basic facts:

• One complete revolution is equal to 360°.
• One complete revolution is also equal to 2π radians.

Since the SI unit of angular displacement is the radian, questions requiring answers in SI units are usually expressed in radians.

The fundamental relationship is:

Angular displacement (θ) = Number of revolutions × 2π

where:

• θ = angular displacement (radians)
• Number of revolutions = total complete turns made by the rotating object
• π ≈ 3.142

This formula is fundamental because every complete revolution corresponds to an angle of 2π radians.

A related formula you will study later is angular velocity:

ω = θ/t

where ω is angular velocity and t is time. Notice that angular velocity cannot be determined until angular displacement is known.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Number of revolutions = 15.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Angular displacement in radians.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given value.

θ = 15 × 2π

= 30π rad.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option A = 15π rad (too small)

Option B = 30π rad (correct)

Option C = 45π rad (too large)

Option D = 60π rad (far too large)

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it assumes one revolution is equal to π radians instead of 2π radians.
Option C is incorrect because it overestimates the angular displacement by multiplying incorrectly.
Option D is incorrect because it doubles the correct value, indicating an extra factor of 2 was introduced.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question gives the number of complete revolutions, immediately recall that one revolution equals 2π radians. Multiply the number of revolutions by 2π unless the question specifically asks for the answer in degrees.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to use 360 instead of 2π when the answer is required in radians. Another common error is forgetting to multiply by the total number of revolutions.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine drawing a circle with a coin on a table. Every time the coin completes one full turn, it has rotated through 2π radians. If it makes 15 complete turns, it rotates through 15 × 2π = 30π radians.

question:
At a building construction site, the drum of a cement mixer rotates through 3¼ complete revolutions while mixing concrete. What is the angular displacement of the drum in radians?

(Take π = 3.14.)

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the angular displacement of a rotating object when the number of complete revolutions is known. The rotating object in this case is the drum of a cement mixer used at a construction site.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this problem, it is important to understand what angular displacement means.

Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its starting position to its final position. Unlike ordinary distance, which measures how far an object moves in a straight or curved path, angular displacement measures how much it has turned.

Many machines such as ceiling fans, grinding machines, turbines, washing machines and cement mixers operate by rotating. Engineers often determine how much these machines have rotated by measuring their angular displacement.

The SI unit of angular displacement is the radian (rad).

The fundamental relationship between revolutions and radians is:

Angular displacement (θ) = Number of revolutions (N) × 2π

This relationship is used because one complete revolution is equal to 360° or 2π radians.

Where:

• θ = angular displacement (radians)
• N = number of complete revolutions
• π ≈ 3.14

Related formula:

Angular velocity, studied later in this topic, is given by:

ω = θ/t

where ω is angular velocity, θ is angular displacement and t is the time taken. This shows that angular displacement is the foundation for understanding angular velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known value.

Number of revolutions, N = 3¼ = 3.25 revolutions.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Angular displacement, θ.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

θ = N × 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

θ = 3.25 × 2 × 3.14

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

2 × 3.14 = 6.28

θ = 3.25 × 6.28

θ = 20.41 rad (approximately).

Step 6: Compare with the options.

20.41 rad corresponds to Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (18.84 rad) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect multiplication or using the wrong number of revolutions.
Option C (21.98 rad) is incorrect because it overestimates the angular displacement and does not equal 3.25 × 2π.
Option D (23.55 rad) is incorrect because it corresponds to a larger angular displacement than that produced by 3¼ revolutions.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question gives the number of revolutions and asks for angular displacement in radians, immediately remember that one revolution equals 2π radians. Multiply the number of revolutions by 2π before choosing your answer. Do not confuse radians with degrees.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine drawing a chalk mark on the outside of the cement mixer drum. Every time the drum makes one complete turn, the mark returns to its starting position after rotating through 2π radians. Since the drum makes 3¼ turns, it rotates through 3¼ × 2π radians, giving approximately 20.41 radians.

question:
A technician is servicing a borehole pumping system in a residential estate. During a maintenance test, the pump shaft completes 9 full revolutions before it is switched off.

What is the angular displacement of the shaft in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question asks you to determine the angular displacement of a rotating borehole pump shaft after it completes a given number of full revolutions.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates about a fixed axis from its starting position to its final position.

Unlike linear displacement, which measures how far an object moves along a path, angular displacement measures how much it has turned.

This concept is important because many machines such as electric motors, turbines, generators, washing machines and borehole pumps operate through rotational motion.

Before studying angular velocity or angular acceleration, it is necessary to understand angular displacement because it describes the amount of rotation.

One complete revolution is equivalent to:

• 360°
• 2π radians

Since the SI unit of angular displacement is the radian, questions involving rotational motion are often answered in radians.

The fundamental relationship is:

θ = n × 2π

where:

• θ = angular displacement (radians)
• n = number of complete revolutions
• π ≈ 3.142

This formula is used whenever the number of revolutions is known and the required quantity is angular displacement in radians.

A related formula studied later is:

ω = θ/t

where ω is angular velocity. This formula uses angular displacement, showing why understanding θ is the foundation of rotational motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Number of revolutions, n = 9.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Angular displacement, θ.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

θ = n × 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given value.

θ = 9 × 2π

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

θ = 18π rad

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

Option B is 18π rad.

Therefore, Option B is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (9π rad) is incorrect because it assumes one revolution is π radians instead of 2π radians.
Option C (27π rad) is incorrect because it results from multiplying by an incorrect conversion factor.
Option D (36π rad) is incorrect because it doubles the correct angular displacement.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always remember the two basic rotational conversions:

• 1 revolution = 360°
• 1 revolution = 2π radians

Whenever the number of revolutions is given and the answer is required in radians, multiply the number of revolutions by 2π.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine drawing a circle with a coin. Every time the coin completes one full turn, it rotates through one complete circle, which is 2π radians. If it turns 9 times, the total rotation is simply 9 × 2π = 18π radians.

question:
Following a prolonged power outage, a secondary school switched on its standby generator to supply electricity during an evening lesson. The maintenance technician recorded that the generator shaft completed 125 revolutions before the public power supply was restored.

What angular displacement did the generator shaft undergo during this period, expressed in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question asks you to determine the angular displacement of a rotating generator shaft after it completes a given number of revolutions. Since the answer is required in radians, you must convert the number of revolutions into angular displacement using the appropriate relationship.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement describes the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its starting position to its final position.

Unlike distance travelled along a path, angular displacement measures rotational change in position.

Before solving questions like this, it is important to understand the relationship between revolutions, degrees and radians.

One complete revolution corresponds to:

• 360°
• 2π radians

Since the SI unit of angular measurement is the radian, most engineering and physics calculations are expressed in radians.

The formula used is:

θ = n × 2π

where:

• θ = angular displacement (radians)
• n = number of complete revolutions

This is a fundamental relationship because every complete revolution always covers an angle of 2π radians.

A related formula you will study next is angular velocity:

ω = θ/t

where ω is angular velocity and t is time. Notice that angular velocity depends on angular displacement, so understanding angular displacement is essential before studying angular velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Number of revolutions, n = 125.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Angular displacement, θ, in radians.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

θ = n × 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given value.

θ = 125 × 2π

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

θ = 250π rad

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

Option C is 250π rad.

Therefore, Option C is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (125π rad) is incorrect because it assumes one revolution equals π radians instead of 2π radians.
Option B (200π rad) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect multiplication of the number of revolutions by 2π.
Option D (500π rad) is incorrect because it doubles the correct angular displacement.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question gives the number of complete revolutions and asks for angular displacement in radians, immediately recall that one revolution equals 2π radians.

A common JAMB mistake is using π instead of 2π for one complete revolution. Always remember:

1 revolution = 360° = 2π radians.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine drawing a circle with a coin on a table. Every time the coin makes one complete turn, it has rotated through 2π radians.

If it turns 125 times, the total angle covered is simply 125 × 2π = 250π radians.

The generator shaft behaves in exactly the same way. Each full rotation contributes another 2π radians to the total angular displacement.

question:
While doing household laundry, a front-loading washing machine drum completes 9 full revolutions and then turns through an additional quarter of a revolution before stopping.

What is the total angular displacement of the drum in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question asks you to determine the total angular displacement of a washing machine drum after completing several full revolutions and an additional fraction of a revolution.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement describes how far a body rotates about a fixed axis from its starting position to its final position.

Unlike linear distance, angular displacement measures rotational change in position. It is one of the basic quantities used to study circular motion.

The SI unit of angular displacement is the radian (rad).

Before solving such questions, remember that:

• One complete revolution = 360° = 2π radians.

Therefore,

Angular displacement (θ) = Number of revolutions × 2π.

Where:

• θ = angular displacement (rad)
• Number of revolutions = total complete and fractional turns made.

This is a fundamental relationship and forms the basis for later topics such as angular velocity (ω = θ/t) and angular acceleration.
steps:
Step 1: Determine the total number of revolutions.

Full revolutions = 9

Additional revolution = 1/4

Total revolutions = 9 + 1/4 = 9.25 revolutions.

Step 2: Apply the angular displacement formula.

θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

Step 3: Substitute the values.

θ = 9.25 × 2π

θ = 18.5π rad.

Step 4: Compare with the options.

Option A gives 18.5π rad, which agrees with the calculated value.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (18π rad) assumes only 9 complete revolutions and ignores the additional quarter revolution.
Option C (18.25π rad) results from an incorrect multiplication of 9.25 by 2.
Option D (19π rad) overestimates the angular displacement and does not correspond to 9.25 revolutions.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to convert only the complete revolutions and forget to include the fractional revolution. Another common error is multiplying by π instead of 2π.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question includes a fraction of a revolution, first convert the entire motion into the total number of revolutions before multiplying by 2π.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine drawing a mark on the washing machine drum. Every complete turn brings the mark back to its starting position, while the extra quarter turn moves it one-fourth of the way around again. Counting all the turns together gives 9.25 revolutions, which corresponds to 18.5π radians.

question:

What is the magnitude of the angular displacement of the carousel in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the angular displacement of a carousel after it completes 3½ revolutions. Since the answer is required in radians, you must convert the number of revolutions into angular displacement using the relationship between a revolution and radians.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

Unlike linear distance, angular displacement describes rotational change in position.

Before solving problems involving angular displacement, it is important to know the standard relationships:

• One complete revolution = 360°
• One complete revolution = 2π radians.

The radian is the SI unit of angular measurement and is commonly used in physics because it naturally relates angles to the geometry of circles.

The formula used is:

Angular displacement (θ) = Number of revolutions × 2π

where:

• θ = angular displacement (radians)
• Number of revolutions = total complete and fractional turns made
• π ≈ 3.142

This formula is fundamental for converting revolutions directly into radians.

A related formula studied later is angular velocity:

ω = θ/t

where ω is angular velocity and t is time. Notice that angular velocity depends on angular displacement, so understanding angular displacement is the first step before studying rotational speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Number of revolutions = 3½ = 3.5 revolutions.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Angular displacement in radians.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given value.

θ = 3.5 × 2π

= 7π radians.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option C gives 7π rad.

Therefore, Option C is the correct answer.

Although the carousel rotates clockwise, the question asks for the magnitude of the angular displacement. Hence, only the size of the angle is required.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (5π rad) is incorrect because it corresponds to only 2½ revolutions.
Option B (6π rad) is incorrect because it represents 3 complete revolutions, not 3½ revolutions.
Option D (8π rad) is incorrect because it corresponds to 4 complete revolutions.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question gives the number of revolutions and asks for angular displacement in radians, immediately recall that one revolution equals 2π radians. explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine drawing a mark on the edge of a merry-go-round. Every time it goes round once, it turns through 2π radians.

If it turns 3½ times, it has turned:

3½ × 2π = 7π radians.

Since only the magnitude is requested, the clockwise direction does not change the answer.

question: A rotary packaging machine in a pharmaceutical manufacturing plant turns through 15 complete revolutions while sealing a batch of medicine containers. What is the total angular displacement of the machine in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question asks you to determine the angular displacement of a rotary packaging machine after it completes 15 full revolutions.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement describes the angle through which a body rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

Unlike linear displacement, which measures movement along a straight or curved path, angular displacement measures rotational change in position.

It is an important quantity in circular motion because it forms the basis for studying angular velocity and angular acceleration.

Before solving questions on angular displacement, remember that one complete revolution corresponds to one full circle.

Useful relationships include:

• One complete revolution = 2π radians

Whenever the number of revolutions is given and the answer is required in radians, use:

Angular displacement, θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

where:

This is a fundamental relationship because every complete revolution always covers an angle of 2π radians.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Number of revolutions = 15.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Angular displacement in radians.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given value.

θ = 15 × 2π

Step 5: Calculate.

θ = 30π rad

Step 6: Compare with the options.

Option C gives 30π rad, which is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it assumes each revolution corresponds to only π radians instead of 2π radians.

explanation.exam_tip:

question:

What is the magnitude of its angular displacement in radians?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to determine the angular displacement of a rotating robotic arm from the number of complete revolutions and express the result in radians.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates about a fixed axis from its initial position to its final position.

Unlike linear displacement, which measures movement along a path, angular displacement measures rotational change in position.

Before solving any angular displacement problem, you should identify:

• the number of revolutions completed;
• whether the answer is required in degrees or radians; and
• whether the question asks for magnitude or direction.

The SI unit of angular displacement is the radian.

One complete revolution corresponds to 360° or 2π radians.

Therefore,

Angular displacement (θ) = Number of revolutions × 2π

where:

• θ = angular displacement (rad)
• Number of revolutions = total turns made
• π ≈ 3.142

This is a fundamental relationship in rotational motion. It is also the foundation for later topics such as angular velocity, given by ω = θ/t, where θ is the angular displacement and t is the time taken.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known value.

Number of revolutions = 2.75.

Step 2: State the required quantity.

Required: Angular displacement in radians.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

Step 4: Substitute the given value.

θ = 2.75 × 2π

= 5.5π rad

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

Option B gives 5.5π rad.

Therefore, Option B is correct.

Although the arm rotated anticlockwise, the question asks for the magnitude of the angular displacement, so only the numerical value is required.

question:
A smart automated production system in a modern manufacturing facility in Ogun State uses a robotic rotary indexing table to move components through different workstations. During a quality assurance inspection, four engineers made the following statements about the angular displacement of the table after it rotates from its initial position:

Engineer I: Angular displacement depends only on the initial and final angular positions of the table, not on the path taken.

Engineer II: If the table completes exactly one full revolution and returns to its starting position, its angular displacement is 360°.

Engineer III: If the table rotates through 1.75 revolutions in one direction, its angular displacement is 3.5π radians.

Engineer IV: Angular displacement is an angular quantity commonly expressed in degrees or radians.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your overall understanding of angular displacement by asking you to assess several statements made about the rotational motion of a robotic indexing table in an automated production system.
explanation.principle:
Angular displacement describes the change in the angular position of a rotating object about a fixed axis.

Imagine a door opening or the hands of a clock moving. Instead of measuring how far a point on the edge travels, angular displacement measures the angle through which the object turns.

It is important because it forms the foundation for studying angular velocity and angular acceleration.

Before solving problems involving angular displacement, remember:

• Angular displacement depends on the change between the initial and final angular positions.
• It is commonly measured in degrees (°) or radians (rad).
• One complete revolution equals 360° or 2π radians.

The fundamental relationship is:

Angular displacement = Number of revolutions × 2π radians

where:

• Angular displacement (θ) is measured in radians.
• Number of revolutions is the total number of complete turns.
• π is approximately 3.142.

A related formula you will study later is angular velocity:

ω = θ/t

where ω is angular velocity, θ is angular displacement and t is time. This shows why understanding angular displacement comes first.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Engineer I.

Angular displacement is determined by comparing the initial and final angular positions. It does not depend on the actual path travelled.

Therefore, Engineer I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Engineer II.

If the table makes one complete revolution and returns to exactly its starting position, its final angular position is the same as its initial position.

Therefore, the angular displacement is 0°, not 360°.

Engineer II is incorrect.

Step 3: Examine Engineer III.

Given:

Number of revolutions = 1.75

Using:

θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

θ = 1.75 × 2π

θ = 3.5π radians.

Engineer III is correct.

Step 4: Examine Engineer IV.

Angular displacement is an angular quantity whose common units are degrees and radians.

Engineer IV is correct.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Correct statements are I, III and IV only.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because Engineer II is wrong. Returning to the starting position gives zero angular displacement.
Option C is incorrect because Engineer II is false while Engineer I and Engineer IV, which are correct, have been omitted.
Option D is incorrect because it includes Engineer II, making the entire combination incorrect.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB often distinguishes between angular displacement and the angle through which an object merely turns.

Always compare the initial and final angular positions.

Remember these key facts:

• One revolution = 360° = 2π radians.
• Returning to the starting position means the angular displacement is zero.
• Degrees and radians are the standard units used to express angular displacement.
explanation.final_answer: B. I, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine rotating a serving tray. If you turn it all the way round and it faces exactly the same direction as before, it has returned to its original position, so its angular displacement is zero.

However, if you stop after one and three-quarter turns, the tray has changed its position by 3.5π radians. This is why Engineers I, III and IV are correct, while Engineer II is not.

question: A ceiling fan in a secondary school classroom rotates continuously during a lesson. Which physical quantity describes how fast the fan turns about its axis?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the physical quantity used to describe how fast an object rotates about a fixed axis.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand the idea of rotational motion.

Many objects around us rotate instead of moving in a straight line. Examples include ceiling fans, bicycle wheels, electric motors and turbines.

When an object rotates, we may ask two different questions:

• How far has it turned?
• How fast is it turning?

The answer to the first question is angular displacement, while the answer to the second question is angular velocity.

Angular velocity is the rate at which angular displacement changes with time. It tells us how quickly an object rotates about its axis.

This concept is important because it helps engineers, technicians and scientists compare the rotational speeds of machines and other rotating systems.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula for angular velocity is:

ω = θ/t

where:

• ω = angular velocity (radians per second, rad s⁻¹)
• θ = angular displacement (radians)
• t = time taken (seconds)

This formula means that angular velocity is obtained by dividing the angle turned by the time taken.

Before studying angular velocity, students should first understand angular displacement, which is given by:

θ = Number of revolutions × 2π

Later in Circular Motion, angular velocity is used to study angular acceleration:

α = Δω/t

and centripetal acceleration:

a = ω²r.
steps:
Step 1: Read what the question is asking.

The question asks for the quantity that describes how fast the ceiling fan rotates.

Step 2: Examine each option.

Option A: Angular displacement describes how far an object has turned, not how fast it turns.

Option B: Angular velocity describes the rate of rotation. This matches the question.

Option C: Angular acceleration describes how angular velocity changes with time.

Option D: Centripetal force is the inward force that keeps an object moving in a circular path. It does not describe rotational speed.

Step 3: Select the correct answer.

The correct quantity is angular velocity.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because angular displacement measures the angle through which an object has rotated, not its speed of rotation.
Option C is incorrect because angular acceleration measures the rate of change of angular velocity, not the rotational speed itself.
Option D is incorrect because centripetal force keeps an object moving in a circular path and is not a measure of how fast it rotates.
explanation.misconception_alert: Many students confuse angular displacement with angular velocity. Remember that angular displacement answers how far an object has rotated, while angular velocity answers how fast it rotates.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a JAMB question contains expressions such as rotates quickly, rotational speed or rate of turning, think of angular velocity. Reserve angular displacement for questions asking how far an object has turned.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two ceiling fans. One completes many turns every minute while the other turns slowly. The faster fan has the greater angular velocity because it rotates more quickly about its axis.

question: A standing electric fan in a living room completes one full revolution in 0.40 s while operating at a constant speed. What is the angular velocity of the fan?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the angular velocity of an object that completes one full revolution in a given time.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what angular velocity means.

Angular velocity is the rate at which an object rotates about a fixed axis. In simple terms, it tells us how fast an object turns.

For example, when an electric fan is switched on, its blades rotate continuously. If the blades complete one revolution in a short time, the fan has a high angular velocity.

Before calculating angular velocity, we must first know the angular displacement.

One complete revolution is equal to 360° or 2π radians.

The fundamental formula for angular velocity is:

ω = θ / t

where:

• ω = angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• θ = angular displacement (rad)
• t = time taken (s)

This formula is fundamental because angular velocity is defined as the rate of change of angular displacement with time.

A related formula you will study later is:

• α = Δω / t

which is used for angular acceleration. Another important relationship is:

• a = ω²r

which is used when studying centripetal acceleration. For this question, only ω = θ / t is required.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Angular displacement, θ = 2π rad (one complete revolution).

Time taken, t = 0.40 s.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Angular velocity, ω.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

ω = θ / t

Step 4: Substitute the values.

ω = 2π / 0.40

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

ω = 5π rad s⁻¹

Step 6: Compare the calculated value with the options.

Option A gives 5π rad s⁻¹.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it underestimates the angular velocity. It would correspond to a longer time than 0.40 s for one revolution.
Option C is incorrect because it results from an incorrect division of 2π by the given time.
Option D is incorrect because it is much smaller than the correct value and indicates a misunderstanding of the angular velocity formula.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to substitute 360 instead of 2π into the formula. Angular velocity in SI units must be expressed using radians, not degrees. Another common error is dividing the time by the angle instead of dividing the angle by the time.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question states that an object completes one full revolution, immediately replace the angular displacement with 2π radians before applying the formula ω = θ / t.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine drawing a complete circle with your finger. One full turn is one revolution, which equals 2π radians. If you complete that turn in only 0.40 s, you are turning very quickly. Dividing the angle turned by the time taken gives an angular velocity of 5π rad s⁻¹.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice and revision.

question: While servicing a grinding machine in a neighbourhood welding workshop, a technician observes that the grinding wheel completes 45 revolutions in 15 s. What is the angular velocity of the grinding wheel?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the angular velocity of a grinding wheel from the number of revolutions it makes within a given time.
explanation.principle:
Angular velocity describes how fast an object rotates about a fixed axis. It tells us the angle through which an object turns every second.

Before studying angular velocity, you should understand angular displacement.

Angular displacement is the total angle through which an object rotates.

One complete revolution corresponds to an angle of 2π radians.

Therefore, if an object makes N revolutions, its angular displacement is:

θ = 2πN

where:
• θ = angular displacement (rad)
• N = number of revolutions

Angular velocity is then obtained by dividing the angular displacement by the time taken:

ω = θ/t

where:
• ω = angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• θ = angular displacement (rad)
• t = time (s)

Combining both relationships gives another useful formula:

ω = 2πN/t

This formula is commonly used whenever the number of revolutions and the time are given directly.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Number of revolutions, N = 45

Time taken, t = 15 s

Step 2: State the required quantity.

Determine the angular velocity, ω.

Step 3: Apply the appropriate formula.

ω = 2πN/t

Step 4: Substitute the values.

ω = (2π × 45)/15

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

ω = 90π/15

ω = 6π rad s⁻¹

Step 6: Verify with the options.

Option B gives 6π rad s⁻¹, which agrees with the calculated value.

Therefore, Option B is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it underestimates the angular velocity. It may result from forgetting to multiply the number of revolutions by 2.
Option C is incorrect because it overestimates the angular velocity. It could arise from an arithmetic error when dividing.
Option D is incorrect because it is twice the correct value, suggesting an incorrect substitution or calculation.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question gives the number of revolutions and the time taken, immediately think of the formula ω = 2πN/t. Always remember that one complete revolution equals 2π radians before performing the calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine drawing a circle with a compass. Every complete turn of the compass is one revolution, which is equal to 2π radians. If the compass completes many turns in a short time, its angular velocity is high. The grinding wheel makes 45 complete turns in 15 seconds, giving an angular velocity of 6π rad s⁻¹.

question: During a cycling exercise, a bicycle wheel turns through an angular displacement of 16 rad in 4 s. What is the angular velocity of the wheel?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the angular velocity of a rotating bicycle wheel when its angular displacement and the time taken are known.
explanation.principle:
Angular velocity describes how fast an object rotates about a fixed axis. It is the rate of change of angular displacement with time.

Before learning angular velocity, it is important to understand angular displacement.

Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates. It is measured in radians (rad).

Angular velocity tells us how quickly that angular displacement occurs. Its SI unit is radians per second (rad s⁻¹).

The fundamental relationship is:

ω = θ/t

where:
• ω = angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• θ = angular displacement (rad)
• t = time taken (s)

This formula is obtained by dividing the angle turned by the time required to turn through that angle.

A related formula you will meet later is:

α = Δω/t

which is used when the angular velocity changes with time. That concept belongs to angular acceleration and should not be confused with angular velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Angular displacement, θ = 16 rad

Time, t = 4 s

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Angular velocity, ω

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

ω = θ/t

Step 4: Substitute the values.

ω = 16/4

Step 5: Calculate the answer.

ω = 4 rad s⁻¹

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

Option B gives 4 rad s⁻¹.

Therefore, Option B is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it underestimates the angular velocity. Dividing 16 by 4 does not give 2.
Option C is incorrect because it doubles the correct value. The calculation 16 ÷ 4 equals 4, not 8.
Option D is incorrect because it is obtained by multiplying instead of dividing the angular displacement by the time.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to multiply the angular displacement by the time instead of dividing by the time. Always remember that angular velocity is a rate, so it is obtained by dividing the angle turned by the time taken.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever angular displacement is given in radians and the time is given in seconds, the quickest approach is to apply ω = θ/t directly. Always check that your final unit is rad s⁻¹.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine turning a bicycle wheel by hand. If the wheel turns through a larger angle in the same amount of time, it has a greater angular velocity. Here, the wheel turns 16 radians in 4 seconds, so it turns at 4 radians every second.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice.

question: A motorcycle wheel rotates with an angular velocity of 8 rad s⁻¹ while travelling along a community road. If the wheel turns through an angular displacement of 40 radians at this constant angular velocity, how long does the motion take?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the time taken for a rotating object when its angular displacement and angular velocity are known.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what angular velocity means.

Angular velocity is the rate at which an object rotates about a fixed axis. It tells us how fast the angle covered by a rotating object changes with time.

In everyday life, many objects rotate, such as motorcycle wheels, ceiling fans, electric motors and turbines. The faster they rotate, the greater their angular velocity.

To understand angular velocity, you should first know angular displacement.

Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates. It is measured in radians (rad).

Angular velocity makes use of angular displacement because it tells us how much angular displacement occurs every second.

The fundamental relationship is:

ω = θ/t

where:

• ω = angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• θ = angular displacement (rad)
• t = time taken (s)

This is a fundamental formula in rotational motion. It is obtained by dividing the angular displacement by the time taken.

When the time is unknown, rearrange the formula:

t = θ/ω

This rearranged form should be used whenever angular displacement and angular velocity are given, but the required quantity is time.

A related formula studied earlier is:

θ = N × 2π

where N is the number of revolutions.

Later in Circular Motion, angular velocity will also be used to determine angular acceleration and centripetal acceleration.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Angular displacement, θ = 40 rad

Angular velocity, ω = 8 rad s⁻¹

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required quantity = Time, t

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

ω = θ/t

Rearrange to make t the subject:

t = θ/ω

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

t = 40/8

Step 5: Calculate.

t = 5 s

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

Option A = 3 s ✗
Option B = 5 s ✓
Option C = 8 s ✗
Option D = 10 s ✗

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because dividing 40 by 8 does not give 3.
Option C is incorrect because it incorrectly assumes the angular velocity is the required time.
Option D is incorrect because it results from an incorrect calculation and does not satisfy the formula t = θ/ω.
explanation.exam_tip: For reverse calculation questions in Circular Motion, first identify the missing quantity before selecting the formula. If time is required, rearrange ω = θ/t to obtain t = θ/ω. Always check that radians cancel, leaving the answer in seconds.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine marking a point on a motorcycle wheel. If the mark turns through a total angle of 40 radians and the wheel rotates at 8 radians every second, it will take 5 seconds to complete that rotation because 40 divided by 8 equals 5.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Angular Velocity for a complete lesson on angular displacement, angular velocity, reverse calculations, unit conversions, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly explanations and additional practice questions.

question: A water pumping machine serving a residential estate rotates at a constant angular velocity of 10 rad s⁻¹. If the machine operates for 9 s, what angular displacement does its rotating shaft cover during this period?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate angular displacement when the angular velocity and the time of rotation are known.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what angular displacement means.

Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates about a fixed axis. It tells us how far the object has turned during its motion.

Imagine watching the rotating shaft of a water pump. As the shaft turns, it sweeps out an angle. That angle is called the angular displacement.

Angular displacement is measured in radians (rad), while time is measured in seconds (s).

To describe how fast the shaft rotates, we use angular velocity.

Angular velocity is the rate at which angular displacement changes with time.

The fundamental relationship connecting these quantities is:

ω = θ/t

where:

• ω = angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• θ = angular displacement (rad)
• t = time (s)

This formula means that angular velocity is obtained by dividing the angle turned by the time taken.

When angular velocity and time are known, the formula can be rearranged to obtain angular displacement:

θ = ωt

This is the appropriate formula whenever the rotational speed remains constant.

Related formulas include:

• θ = N × 2π, where N is the number of revolutions.

• ω = 2πf, where f is the rotational frequency in revolutions per second.

These relationships all describe different ways of analysing rotational motion.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known values.

Angular velocity, ω = 10 rad s⁻¹

Time, t = 9 s

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

The question asks for the angular displacement, θ.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

θ = ωt

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

θ = 10 × 9

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

θ = 90 rad

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

90 rad corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (45 rad) is incorrect because it is only half of the correct angular displacement.
Option C (100 rad) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect multiplication.
Option D (190 rad) is incorrect because it greatly overestimates the angle turned during the given time.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to divide angular velocity by time instead of multiplying by time when calculating angular displacement. Remember that θ = ωt is obtained by rearranging ω = θ/t.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever you know the angular velocity and the time, think immediately of the formula θ = ωt. Always check that angular velocity is in rad s⁻¹ and time is in seconds before substituting into the formula.
explanation.simple_explanation: Suppose a clock's second hand could rotate much faster than normal. If you know how many radians it turns every second and how long it keeps turning, multiplying the two tells you the total angle it has covered. In the same way, the rotating shaft of the water pumping machine covers 90 radians in 9 seconds.
